explanation: The requirement is: if either prop_0 (like prop_1) or prop_2 occurs, then both prop_3 and prop_4 must occur (i.e., after the condition, both should hold). This can be expressed globally: whenever (prop_0 or prop_2) is true, then (prop_3 and prop_4) must be true.

The requirement states that whenever either prop_0 or prop_2 becomes true, prop_3 should also be true (probably at the same time or right afterwards, depending on interpretation). Secondly, whenever prop_4 becomes true, it should remain continuously true (it should not be 'interrupted', i.e., there should not be a state where it is false after it becomes true). This is formalized by stating that once prop_4 becomes true, it stays true globally from that point on.

The formula requires that whenever either 'prop_0 or prop_1' occurs, or 'prop_2' occurs, then 'prop_3' and 'prop_4' must hold. In temporal logic, this is most cleanly stated as: globally, if either (prop_0 or prop_1 or prop_2) holds, then (prop_3 and prop_4) should hold at the same instant. This interpretation treats 'any ongoing prop_4' as 'prop_4 holds whenever the left side holds'.

The formula translates as: if either prop_0 (such as prop_1) or prop_2 holds, then prop_3 must hold as well; meanwhile, if prop_4 ever holds, it should never become false (i.e., prop_4 is never interrupted). This is achieved by requiring prop_3 to hold whenever the triggering condition holds, and prop_4 to always hold globally after it starts holding.

The sentence states that if either prop_0 or prop_1 occurs, then prop_2 must occur (possibly as a response). In addition, it says that any prop_3 shall not be interrupted, which we interpret in temporal logic as "whenever prop_3 holds, it continues to hold in the next state as well" – i.e., prop_3 is never interrupted once it starts. This can be formalized in LTL as (prop_3 -> X prop_3) globally. The first part can be formalized as globally: if prop_0 or prop_1 occurs, then eventually prop_2 will occur.

The requirement can be broken into two parts: (1) If prop_0 or prop_2 holds, then prop_3 must hold at the same time. (2) If prop_4 holds, then prop_5 must not hold at the same time. Both are safety requirements expressing invariants, so we encapsulate both with the globally (G) operator. The implication is expressed with ->, and 'shall not be' with !.

The statement says: If either prop_0 or prop_2 occurs, then both prop_3 and 'any ongoing prop_4' shall hold. The phrase 'any ongoing prop_4' suggests that if prop_4 was true before, it should not be violated after the triggering condition (prop_0 or prop_2). This is encoded by requiring that (prop_3 & prop_4) hold once prop_0 or prop_2 becomes true, and globally if (prop_0 or prop_2) is ever true, prop_3 and (if prop_4 was true just before) it remains true.

This requirement specifies that whenever 'prop_0 or prop_2' becomes true, 'prop_3' and any ongoing 'prop_4' must also be true at that same time. In LTL, this is typically modeled as: globally, if ('prop_0 or prop_2') is true, then ('prop_3' is true and, if 'prop_4' is true now, then it must remain true). This can be formalized with a release (R) operator to indicate 'prop_4' must persist as long as it was ongoing at the trigger point.

If either prop_0 or prop_1 is true, then prop_2 and prop_3 must both be true at the same time. This is expressed as a safety property: globally, when (prop_0 or prop_1) holds, then (prop_2 and prop_3) holds.

The sentence states that if either (prop_0 or prop_1) or prop_2 holds, then both prop_3 and prop_4 should hold. This describes a global property of the system: everywhere, whenever any of (prop_0 or prop_1 or prop_2) is true, (prop_3 and prop_4) must also be true.
